Relevance — Definition.rule: Evidence is relevant if it has any tendency to make a fact of consequence more or less probable than it would be without the evidence.
Relevance — Definition.application: Because the evidence makes a fact of consequence more probable, it is relevant.

Relevance — Admissibility.rule: Relevant evidence is admissible unless excluded by the Constitution, a statute, the Federal Rules of Evidence, or other rules.
Relevance — Admissibility.application: Because no rule excludes the evidence, it is admissible.

Irrelevant Evidence.application: Because the evidence is irrelevant, it is excluded.

Rule 403 — Balancing Test.rule: Relevant evidence may be excluded if its probative value is substantially outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice, confusion, or misleading the jury.
Rule 403 — Balancing Test.application: Because unfair prejudice substantially outweighs probative value, the evidence is excluded.

Rule 403 — Other Considerations.rule: Relevant evidence may also be excluded for undue delay, wasting time, or needless presentation of cumulative evidence.
Rule 403 — Other Considerations.application: Because the evidence is cumulative, it may be excluded.

Policy Exclusion — Subsequent Remedial M.rule: Evidence of subsequent remedial measures is not admissible to prove negligence, culpable conduct, or product defect.
Policy Exclusion — Subsequent Remedial M.application: Because the evidence concerns repairs after the accident, it is excluded.

Subsequent Remedial Measures — Permitted.rule: Evidence of subsequent remedial measures may be admitted to prove ownership, control, feasibility, or impeachment.
Subsequent Remedial Measures — Permitted.application: Because ownership is disputed, the evidence is admissible for that purpose.

Policy Exclusion — Settlement Offers.rule: Evidence of settlement offers or negotiations is not admissible to prove liability or the amount of a claim.
Policy Exclusion — Settlement Offers.application: Because the evidence relates to settlement discussions, it is excluded.

Settlement — Permitted Uses.rule: Evidence of settlement negotiations may be admitted for purposes such as impeachment or proving bias.
Settlement — Permitted Uses.application: Because the evidence shows bias, it may be admitted.

Policy Exclusion — Offers to Pay Medical.rule: Evidence of offers to pay medical expenses is not admissible to prove liability for an injury.
Policy Exclusion — Offers to Pay Medical.application: Because the statement offered to pay medical bills, it is excluded.

Policy Exclusion — Pleas and Plea Discus.rule: Evidence of withdrawn guilty pleas, nolo contendere pleas, and plea discussions is not admissible against the defendant.
Policy Exclusion — Pleas and Plea Discus.application: Because the statement occurred during plea discussions, it is excluded.

Policy Exclusion — Liability Insurance.rule: Evidence that a person was insured against liability is not admissible to prove negligence or wrongful conduct.
Policy Exclusion — Liability Insurance.application: Because insurance was offered to show negligence, it is excluded.

Liability Insurance — Permitted Uses.rule: Evidence of liability insurance may be admitted to prove ownership, control, or bias.
Liability Insurance — Permitted Uses.application: Because control is disputed, the evidence is admissible.

Character Evidence — General Rule.rule: Character evidence is not admissible to prove that a person acted in accordance with a character trait on a particular occasion.
Character Evidence — General Rule.application: Because the evidence was offered to show action in conformity, it is inadmissible.

Character Evidence — Criminal Defendant.rule: In a criminal case, a defendant may offer evidence of a pertinent character trait, and the prosecution may rebut that evidence.
Character Evidence — Criminal Defendant.application: Because the defendant introduced character evidence, the prosecution may rebut.

Character Evidence — Victim in Criminal.rule: In a criminal case, a defendant may offer evidence of a pertinent character trait of the victim, and the prosecution may rebut or offer evidence of the defendant’s same trait.
Character Evidence — Victim in Criminal.application: Because the defendant attacked the victim’s character, rebuttal is permitted.

Character Evidence — Homicide Case.rule: In a homicide case, the prosecution may offer evidence of the victim’s peaceful character to rebut evidence that the victim was the first aggressor.
Character Evidence — Homicide Case.application: Because the defendant claimed the victim was the aggressor, peaceful character evidence is admissible.

Character Evidence — Methods of Proof.rule: Character may be proven by reputation testimony or opinion testimony, and by specific instances of conduct only on cross-examination.
Character Evidence — Methods of Proof.application: Because the testimony was opinion evidence, it is an acceptable method of proof.

Character Evidence — Civil Cases.rule: Character evidence is generally not admissible in civil cases unless character is an essential element of a claim or defense.
Character Evidence — Civil Cases.application: Because character was not an essential element, the evidence is inadmissible.

Other Acts Evidence — Rule 404(b).rule: Evidence of other crimes, wrongs, or acts is not admissible to prove character but may be admitted for other purposes such as motive, intent, or identity.
Other Acts Evidence — Rule 404(b).application: Because the evidence was offered to show intent, it may be admitted.

Other Acts Evidence — Permitted Purposes.rule: Other acts evidence may be admitted to prove motive, opportunity, intent, preparation, plan, knowledge, identity, or absence of mistake.
Other Acts Evidence — Permitted Purposes.application: Because the evidence shows a common plan, it is admissible.

Other Acts Evidence — Notice Requirement.rule: In a criminal case, the prosecution must provide reasonable notice of its intent to introduce other acts evidence.
Other Acts Evidence — Notice Requirement.application: Because notice was provided, the evidence may be admitted.

Habit Evidence.rule: Evidence of a person’s habit or an organization’s routine practice is admissible to prove conduct in conformity with the habit or routine.
Habit Evidence.application: Because the behavior was habitual, the evidence is admissible.

Impeachment — Purpose.rule: Impeachment is the process of challenging the credibility of a witness.
Impeachment — Purpose.application: Because the evidence challenges credibility, it is proper impeachment.

Impeachment — Who May Impeach.rule: Any party, including the party that called the witness, may impeach a witness.
Impeachment — Who May Impeach.application: Because a party may impeach its own witness, impeachment is permitted.

Impeachment — Bias or Interest.rule: A witness may be impeached by showing bias, interest, or motive to lie.
Impeachment — Bias or Interest.application: Because the witness had a motive to lie, bias impeachment applies.

Impeachment — Prior Inconsistent Stateme.rule: A witness may be impeached by a prior inconsistent statement if the witness is given an opportunity to explain or deny the statement.
Impeachment — Prior Inconsistent Stateme.application: Because the witness made a conflicting statement, impeachment is proper.

Prior Inconsistent Statement — Extrinsic.rule: Extrinsic evidence of a prior inconsistent statement is admissible only if the witness is given an opportunity to explain or deny the statement.
Prior Inconsistent Statement — Extrinsic.application: Because the witness was confronted, extrinsic evidence is admissible.

Impeachment — Sensory or Mental Defects.rule: A witness may be impeached by showing defects in perception, memory, or narration.
Impeachment — Sensory or Mental Defects.application: Because the witness had impaired perception, credibility is weakened.

Impeachment — Character for Truthfulness.rule: A witness’s character for truthfulness may be attacked or supported by reputation or opinion testimony.
Impeachment — Character for Truthfulness.application: Because the witness’s honesty was attacked, character evidence is admissible.

Impeachment — Specific Instances of Cond.rule: Specific instances of conduct may be inquired into on cross-examination if probative of truthfulness but may not be proven by extrinsic evidence.
Impeachment — Specific Instances of Cond.application: Because the conduct reflects truthfulness, questioning is permitted on cross.

Impeachment — Criminal Convictions.rule: A witness may be impeached by evidence of certain criminal convictions.
Impeachment — Criminal Convictions.application: Because the witness has a qualifying conviction, impeachment is allowed.

Criminal Convictions — Crimes Involving.rule: Evidence of a crime involving dishonesty or false statement must be admitted for impeachment purposes.
Criminal Convictions — Crimes Involving.application: Because the conviction involved dishonesty, impeachment is mandatory.

Criminal Convictions — Felonies.rule: Felony convictions may be admitted to impeach a witness if the probative value outweighs the prejudicial effect.
Criminal Convictions — Felonies.application: Because probative value outweighed prejudice, the conviction was admitted.

Criminal Convictions — Time Limit.rule: Convictions more than ten years old are admissible only if their probative value substantially outweighs their prejudicial effect.
Criminal Convictions — Time Limit.application: Because the conviction was old and prejudicial, it was excluded.

Impeachment — Contradiction.rule: A witness may be impeached by contradiction when evidence shows that the witness’s testimony is factually incorrect.
Impeachment — Contradiction.application: Because evidence contradicted the testimony, impeachment is proper.

Rehabilitation.rule: A witness may be rehabilitated after impeachment by evidence explaining or countering the impeachment.
Rehabilitation.application: Because the impeachment was attacked, rehabilitation is permitted.

Hearsay — Definition.rule: Hearsay is an out-of-court statement offered to prove the truth of the matter asserted.
Hearsay — Definition.application: Because the statement was made out of court and offered for its truth, it is hearsay.

Statement — Definition.rule: A statement is a person’s oral assertion, written assertion, or nonverbal conduct intended as an assertion.
Statement — Definition.application: Because the conduct was intended as an assertion, it qualifies as a statement.

Declarant — Definition.rule: The declarant is the person who made the statement.
Declarant — Definition.application: Because the witness made the statement, the witness is the declarant.

Offered for the Truth.rule: A statement is offered for the truth when its relevance depends on the statement being true.
buzzwords: depends on the statement being true
Offered for the Truth.application: Because the statement’s value depends on its truth, it is offered for the truth.

Not Hearsay — Verbal Acts.rule: Statements that have independent legal significance, such as words of contract formation or defamation, are not hearsay.
Not Hearsay — Verbal Acts.application: Because the words themselves create legal rights, the statement is not hearsay.

Not Hearsay — Effect on Listener.rule: Statements offered to show their effect on the listener are not hearsay.
Not Hearsay — Effect on Listener.application: Because the statement explains the listener’s behavior, it is not hearsay.

Not Hearsay — State of Mind.rule: Statements offered to show the declarant’s state of mind, such as intent or motive, are not hearsay.
Not Hearsay — State of Mind.application: Because the statement shows intent, it is not hearsay.

Not Hearsay — Impeachment.rule: Prior statements offered solely to impeach a witness are not hearsay.
Not Hearsay — Impeachment.application: Because the statement was offered to impeach, it is not hearsay.

Non-Hearsay — Prior Inconsistent Stateme.rule: A prior inconsistent statement is not hearsay if it was made under oath at a trial, hearing, or deposition.
Non-Hearsay — Prior Inconsistent Stateme.application: Because the statement was sworn testimony, it is not hearsay.

Non-Hearsay — Prior Consistent Statement.rule: A prior consistent statement is not hearsay when offered to rebut a charge of recent fabrication or improper influence.
Non-Hearsay — Prior Consistent Statement.application: Because the statement rebuts a charge of fabrication, it is not hearsay.

Non-Hearsay — Statement of Party Opponen.rule: A statement made by a party and offered against that party is not hearsay.
Non-Hearsay — Statement of Party Opponen.application: Because the statement was made by the opposing party, it is admissible.

Party Opponent — Adoptive Admission.rule: An adoptive admission occurs when a party manifests belief in the truth of another person’s statement.
Party Opponent — Adoptive Admission.application: Because the party adopted the statement, it is not hearsay.

Party Opponent — Authorized Statement.rule: A statement made by a person authorized by the party to speak on the subject is not hearsay.
Party Opponent — Authorized Statement.application: Because the speaker was authorized, the statement is admissible.

Party Opponent — Agent or Employee.rule: A statement made by an agent or employee concerning a matter within the scope of the relationship during its existence is not hearsay.
Party Opponent — Agent or Employee.application: Because the statement was within the scope of employment, it is admissible.

Party Opponent — Co-Conspirator Statemen.rule: A statement made by a co-conspirator during and in furtherance of the conspiracy is not hearsay.
Party Opponent — Co-Conspirator Statemen.application: Because the statement furthered the conspiracy, it is admissible.

Present Sense Impression.rule: A present sense impression is a statement describing or explaining an event or condition made while or immediately after the declarant perceived it.
Present Sense Impression.application: Because the statement described the event as it occurred, it qualifies as a present sense impression.

Excited Utterance.rule: An excited utterance is a statement relating to a startling event made while the declarant was under the stress of excitement caused by the event.
Excited Utterance.application: Because the declarant was still under stress from the event, the statement is an excited utterance.

Then-Existing Mental, Emotional, or Phys.rule: A statement of the declarant’s then-existing state of mind, emotion, sensation, or physical condition is admissible to prove that condition.
Then-Existing Mental, Emotional, or Phys.application: Because the statement reflected the declarant’s present intent, it is admissible.

Then-Existing Condition — Exclusion of M.rule: Statements of memory or belief are not admissible to prove the fact remembered or believed.
Then-Existing Condition — Exclusion of M.application: Because the statement recalled a past fact, it is excluded.

Statements for Medical Diagnosis or Trea.rule: Statements made for purposes of medical diagnosis or treatment describing medical history, symptoms, or cause are admissible.
Statements for Medical Diagnosis or Trea.application: Because the statement was made to obtain treatment, it is admissible.

Recorded Recollection.rule: A recorded recollection is admissible when a witness once had knowledge, now lacks sufficient recollection, and made or adopted a record when the matter was fresh.
Recorded Recollection.application: Because the witness cannot recall and the record was made when fresh, it may be read into evidence.

Business Records.rule: Records of a business activity are admissible if made at or near the time by someone with knowledge and kept in the regular course of business.
Business Records.application: Because the record was kept routinely and timely, it qualifies as a business record.

Business Records — Foundation Witness.rule: The foundation for a business record may be established by a custodian or other qualified witness.
Business Records — Foundation Witness.application: Because a custodian testified, the foundation is satisfied.

Public Records.rule: Public records setting forth the activities of an office or matters observed pursuant to a legal duty are admissible.
Public Records.application: Because the record was prepared pursuant to legal duty, it is admissible.

Public Records — Law Enforcement Limitat.rule: In criminal cases, matters observed by law enforcement personnel are not admissible against the defendant.
Public Records — Law Enforcement Limitat.application: Because the record was prepared by police, it is excluded.

Past Recollection Recorded — Use at Tria.rule: A recorded recollection may be read into evidence but may not be received as an exhibit unless offered by an adverse party.
Past Recollection Recorded — Use at Tria.application: Because the rule permits reading only, the document itself is not admitted.

Absence of Business or Public Record.rule: Evidence that a matter is not included in business or public records may be admitted to prove the matter did not occur.
Absence of Business or Public Record.application: Because no record exists where one should, nonoccurrence is shown.

Learned Treatises.rule: Statements in learned treatises may be read into evidence if established as reliable authority and relied upon by an expert.
Learned Treatises.application: Because the treatise was reliable and relied upon, it may be read to the jury.

Declarant Unavailable — Definition.rule: A declarant is unavailable when the declarant is exempted by privilege, refuses to testify, lacks memory, is dead or ill, or cannot be procured by reasonable means.
Declarant Unavailable — Definition.application: Because the declarant could not be procured despite reasonable efforts, the declarant is unavailable.

Former Testimony.rule: Former testimony is admissible if the declarant is unavailable and the testimony was given under oath with an opportunity for cross-examination by a party with similar motive.
Former Testimony.application: Because the opposing party previously cross-examined with similar motive, the testimony is admissible.

Statement Against Interest.rule: A statement against interest is admissible when a declarant makes a statement so contrary to the declarant’s pecuniary, proprietary, or penal interest that a reasonable person would not have made it unless true.
Statement Against Interest.application: Because the statement exposed the declarant to criminal liability, it qualifies as a statement against interest.

Statement Against Interest — Corroborati.rule: In criminal cases, a statement against penal interest offered to exculpate the accused must be supported by corroborating circumstances indicating trustworthiness.
Statement Against Interest — Corroborati.application: Because corroborating evidence supports the statement, it may be admitted.

Dying Declaration.rule: A dying declaration is admissible when the declarant believes death is imminent and the statement concerns the cause or circumstances of death.
Dying Declaration.application: Because the declarant believed death was imminent, the statement is admissible.

Dying Declaration — Case Limitation.rule: Dying declarations are admissible only in homicide prosecutions or civil cases.
Dying Declaration — Case Limitation.application: Because the case is a homicide prosecution, the dying declaration is admissible.

Statement of Personal or Family History.rule: Statements concerning a declarant’s own birth, marriage, divorce, ancestry, or similar family history are admissible when the declarant is unavailable.
Statement of Personal or Family History.application: Because the statement concerns family history, it is admissible.

Forfeiture by Wrongdoing.rule: A party forfeits the right to object to hearsay when the party intentionally causes the declarant’s unavailability to prevent testimony.
Forfeiture by Wrongdoing.application: Because the defendant caused the witness’s absence, the hearsay objection is forfeited.

Confrontation Clause — General Rule.rule: In a criminal case, the Sixth Amendment Confrontation Clause prohibits the admission of testimonial hearsay unless the declarant is unavailable and the defendant had a prior opportunity to cross-examine.
Confrontation Clause — General Rule.application: Because the defendant had no prior opportunity to cross-examine, the testimonial statement is excluded.

Testimonial Statements.rule: Testimonial statements include statements made during police interrogation or with the primary purpose of establishing evidence for prosecution.
Testimonial Statements.application: Because the statement was made to police for prosecution, it is testimonial.

Non-Testimonial Statements.rule: Non-testimonial statements are not subject to the Confrontation Clause and are governed only by hearsay rules.
Non-Testimonial Statements.application: Because the statement was non-testimonial, the Confrontation Clause does not apply.

Emergency Doctrine.rule: Statements made to assist police during an ongoing emergency are non-testimonial.
Emergency Doctrine.application: Because the statement addressed an ongoing emergency, it is non-testimonial.

Forfeiture by Wrongdoing — Confrontation.rule: A defendant forfeits Confrontation Clause rights by intentionally causing a witness’s unavailability to prevent testimony.
Forfeiture by Wrongdoing — Confrontation.application: Because the defendant caused the witness’s absence, confrontation rights are forfeited.

Dying Declarations — Confrontation Claus.rule: Dying declarations are admissible even if testimonial and do not violate the Confrontation Clause.
Dying Declarations — Confrontation Claus.application: Because the statement was a dying declaration, it is admissible.

Attorney-Client Privilege.rule: The attorney-client privilege protects confidential communications between an attorney and client made for the purpose of obtaining legal advice.
Attorney-Client Privilege.application: Because the communication sought legal advice, it is privileged.

Attorney-Client Privilege — Waiver.rule: The attorney-client privilege is waived if the communication is disclosed to a third party.
Attorney-Client Privilege — Waiver.application: Because the communication was shared, the privilege is waived.

Work Product Doctrine.rule: The work product doctrine protects materials prepared in anticipation of litigation from disclosure.
Work Product Doctrine.application: Because the document was prepared for litigation, it is protected.

Work Product — Opinion Work Product.rule: Opinion work product reflecting an attorney’s mental impressions or legal theories receives heightened protection.
Work Product — Opinion Work Product.application: Because the material reflects legal strategy, it is highly protected.

Spousal Privilege — Testimonial.rule: The spousal testimonial privilege allows a spouse to refuse to testify against the other spouse in a criminal case.
Spousal Privilege — Testimonial.application: Because the witness is a spouse, testimony may be refused.

Spousal Communications Privilege.rule: The spousal communications privilege protects confidential communications made between spouses during the marriage.
Spousal Communications Privilege.application: Because the statement was made privately during marriage, it is privileged.

Physician-Patient Privilege.rule: The physician-patient privilege protects confidential communications made for purposes of medical diagnosis or treatment.
Physician-Patient Privilege.application: Because the communication was for treatment, it is privileged.

Psychotherapist-Patient Privilege.rule: The psychotherapist-patient privilege protects confidential communications made for purposes of mental health treatment.
Psychotherapist-Patient Privilege.application: Because the communication concerned therapy, it is privileged.

Clergy-Penitent Privilege.rule: The clergy-penitent privilege protects confidential communications made to a clergy member for spiritual counseling.
Clergy-Penitent Privilege.application: Because the statement was made for spiritual guidance, it is privileged.

Authentication — General Rule.rule: To authenticate evidence, the proponent must produce sufficient evidence to support a finding that the item is what the proponent claims it is.
Authentication — General Rule.application: Because sufficient evidence shows the item is authentic, it may be admitted.

Authentication — Testimony of a Witness.rule: Evidence may be authenticated by testimony of a witness with knowledge that the item is what it is claimed to be.
Authentication — Testimony of a Witness.application: Because a knowledgeable witness testified, authentication is satisfied.

Authentication — Non-Expert Opinion on H.rule: A non-expert may authenticate handwriting based on familiarity not acquired for purposes of litigation.
Authentication — Non-Expert Opinion on H.application: Because the witness was familiar with the handwriting beforehand, authentication is proper.

Authentication — Comparison by Trier or.rule: Handwriting may be authenticated by comparison by an expert witness or the trier of fact with an authenticated specimen.
Authentication — Comparison by Trier or.application: Because the signature matches an authenticated exemplar, authentication is satisfied.

Authentication — Distinctive Characteris.rule: Evidence may be authenticated by its appearance, contents, substance, internal patterns, or other distinctive characteristics.
Authentication — Distinctive Characteris.application: Because the message contained unique details, it is authenticated.

Authentication — Chain of Custody.rule: Physical evidence may be authenticated by leading evidence of a chain of custody sufficient to show no material alteration.
Authentication — Chain of Custody.application: Because custody was properly tracked, the evidence is authenticated.

Authentication — Voice Identification.rule: A voice may be authenticated by opinion based on hearing the voice at any time under circumstances connecting it to the speaker.
Authentication — Voice Identification.application: Because the witness recognized the voice, authentication is satisfied.

Authentication — Telephone Conversations.rule: Telephone conversations may be authenticated by evidence that the call was made to the number assigned to a particular person or business.
Authentication — Telephone Conversations.application: Because the call was placed to the listed number, authentication is satisfied.

Self-Authentication — Definition.rule: Self-authenticating evidence requires no extrinsic evidence of authenticity to be admitted.
Self-Authentication — Definition.application: Because the document is self-authenticating, no foundation is required.

Self-Authentication — Public Documents.rule: Certified copies of public records are self-authenticating.
Self-Authentication — Public Documents.application: Because the record was certified, it is self-authenticating.

Self-Authentication — Business Records (.rule: Certified business records are self-authenticating if accompanied by a proper certification.
Self-Authentication — Business Records (.application: Because the record was properly certified, it is self-authenticating.

Best Evidence Rule — General Rule.rule: The best evidence rule requires the original writing, recording, or photograph to prove its content.
Best Evidence Rule — General Rule.application: Because the content of the writing is at issue, the original is required.

Best Evidence Rule — When Applicable.rule: The best evidence rule applies only when a party seeks to prove the contents of a writing, recording, or photograph.
Best Evidence Rule — When Applicable.application: Because the contents are not at issue, the rule does not apply.

Original — Definition.rule: An original includes the writing or recording itself and any counterpart intended to have the same effect.
Original — Definition.application: Because the duplicate was intended as an original, it qualifies.

Duplicates.rule: A duplicate is admissible to the same extent as an original unless there is a genuine question about authenticity or it would be unfair.
Duplicates.application: Because no authenticity issue exists, the duplicate is admissible.

Best Evidence — Exceptions.rule: Other evidence of content is admissible when the original is lost or destroyed, cannot be obtained, is in the opponent’s possession, or is not closely related to a controlling issue.
Best Evidence — Exceptions.application: Because the original was lost, secondary evidence is admissible.

Lay Opinion Testimony.rule: Lay opinion testimony is admissible if it is rationally based on the witness’s perception, helpful to understanding the testimony, and not based on scientific or technical knowledge.
Lay Opinion Testimony.application: Because the opinion was based on perception and helpful, it is admissible.

Expert Testimony — General Rule.rule: Expert testimony is admissible if the expert is qualified and the testimony will help the trier of fact understand the evidence or determine a fact in issue.
Expert Testimony — General Rule.application: Because the expert was qualified and helpful, the testimony is admissible.

Expert Qualification.rule: An expert may be qualified by knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education.
Expert Qualification.application: Because the witness had specialized experience, the expert was qualified.

Expert Testimony — Reliability.rule: Expert testimony must be based on sufficient facts or data and reliable principles and methods reliably applied to the facts of the case.
Expert Testimony — Reliability.application: Because the expert used reliable methods, the testimony is admissible.

Bases of Expert Opinion.rule: An expert may base an opinion on facts or data personally observed or made known to the expert, including inadmissible evidence reasonably relied upon by experts in the field.
Bases of Expert Opinion.application: Because experts reasonably rely on such data, the opinion is permitted.

Disclosure of Inadmissible Facts.rule: Inadmissible facts underlying an expert’s opinion may be disclosed to the jury only if their probative value substantially outweighs their prejudicial effect.
Disclosure of Inadmissible Facts.application: Because probative value outweighed prejudice, disclosure was allowed.

Ultimate Issue.rule: Expert testimony is not objectionable merely because it embraces an ultimate issue to be decided by the trier of fact.
Ultimate Issue.application: Because the testimony addressed an ultimate issue, it is admissible.

Expert Opinion — Criminal Mental State L.rule: An expert may not state an opinion about whether a criminal defendant had the required mental state constituting an element of the crime.
Expert Opinion — Criminal Mental State L.application: Because the expert opined on intent, the testimony is excluded.

Judicial Notice — Adjudicative Facts.rule: A court may take judicial notice of an adjudicative fact that is not subject to reasonable dispute.
Judicial Notice — Adjudicative Facts.application: Because the fact is indisputable, judicial notice is proper.

Judicial Notice — Sources.rule: A fact may be judicially noticed if it is generally known or can be accurately and readily determined from reliable sources.
Judicial Notice — Sources.application: Because the fact is verifiable from reliable sources, notice is proper.

Judicial Notice — Civil vs Criminal Case.rule: In a civil case, the jury must accept a judicially noticed fact as conclusive, while in a criminal case the jury may accept or reject it.
Judicial Notice — Civil vs Criminal Case.application: Because the case is criminal, the jury may reject the noticed fact.
